surgery_page.title: Expert Surgery in Kharkiv
surgery_page.cta: Book a Consultation with the Professor
surgery_page.q1: Which equipment is used for laparoscopy?
surgery_page.a1: MARIA Medical Center uses Karl Storz laparoscopic equipment. The specific instruments depend on the procedure.
surgery_page.q2: Can a biopsy be taken during laparoscopy?
surgery_page.a2: Yes. When indicated, the surgeon can obtain a tissue sample under direct visualization and send it for pathological examination.
surgery_page.q3: Which hernias are treated laparoscopically and which by open surgery?
surgery_page.a3: The approach is selected individually based on hernia type and size, previous operations, general condition, and surgical expertise.
surgery_page.a4: This depends on the suture material. Absorbable sutures usually do not require removal; follow the discharge instructions.
surgery_page.q5: Are removed lesions sent for pathology?
surgery_page.a5: Removed tissue is generally sent for pathological examination. The final decision depends on the procedure and clinical situation.
surgery_page.q6: How can I confirm the surgical consultation terms?
surgery_page.a6: Confirm current terms when booking by phone or through an available messenger.
surgery_page.q7: How long does laparoscopic surgery take?
surgery_page.a7: Duration depends on diagnosis, extent, and complexity. The surgeon can estimate it after evaluation.
surgery_page.q8: How long is recovery after laparoscopy?
surgery_page.a8: Recovery depends on the operation, patient condition, and type of work.
surgery_page.q9: When can I return to work after laparoscopy?
surgery_page.a9: Timing depends on procedure extent and physical demands and is advised by the surgeon.
surgery_page.q10: Is general anesthesia required for laparoscopy?
surgery_page.a10: Most laparoscopic procedures use general anesthesia. The anesthesiologist selects the method.
surgery_page.q11: Which complications can occur after laparoscopy?
surgery_page.a11: Risks depend on the procedure and patient and are discussed before surgery.

oncosurgery_page.title: Oncologic Surgery in Kharkiv
oncosurgery_page.q1: Is an oncologist’s referral required for surgical consultation?
oncosurgery_page.a1: No. A referral is not mandatory. Bring any available examination results.
oncosurgery_page.q2: Can cancer be treated without surgery?
oncosurgery_page.a2: This depends on tumor type, stage, and sensitivity to systemic or radiation therapy. The decision should be multidisciplinary.
oncosurgery_page.q3: What should I bring to an oncologic surgeon consultation?
oncosurgery_page.a3: Bring CT or MRI reports and images, biopsy results, laboratory tests, ultrasound reports, and discharge summaries. The physician can arrange missing examinations.
oncosurgery_page.q4: How can I obtain the cost of cancer surgery?
oncosurgery_page.a4: Cost depends on diagnosis, procedure extent, materials, and additional treatment stages. An estimate is possible after evaluation.
oncosurgery_page.q5: How quickly can surgery be scheduled?
oncosurgery_page.a5: Timing depends on urgency, patient condition, completed tests, and operating-room availability. Emergency symptoms require immediate care.
oncosurgery_page.q6: Can cancer surgery be performed laparoscopically?
oncosurgery_page.a6: For some tumors, a minimally invasive approach is appropriate and guideline-concordant. Suitability depends on stage, location, and general condition.
oncosurgery_page.q7: How should I prepare for tumor surgery?
oncosurgery_page.a7: Preparation is individualized and may include tests, imaging, anesthesia assessment, and medication adjustment. Do not stop medicines without medical advice.
oncosurgery_page.q8: Is drug treatment needed after surgery?
oncosurgery_page.a8: The decision depends on stage, pathology, biomarkers, and general condition.
oncosurgery_page.a9: Prognosis is individual and is discussed after complete staging.
oncosurgery_page.q10: How often are follow-up visits needed after cancer surgery?
oncosurgery_page.a10: The oncologist prepares an individual follow-up plan based on current guidance.
oncosurgery_page.q11: What should I do if metastases are found?
oncosurgery_page.a11: Consult the oncology team to assess all available treatment options.

gynecology_page.title: Gynecology in Kharkiv
gynecology_page.q1: How should I prepare for a gynecology appointment?
gynecology_page.a1: If the situation is not urgent, confirm the most suitable examination time when booking. Bring previous ultrasound reports, test results, and a medication list.
gynecology_page.q2: How often should I see a gynecologist?
gynecology_page.a2: The frequency of preventive visits depends on age, medical history, and risk factors. A physician should recommend an individual schedule.
gynecology_page.q3: Does Professor Sushkov see patients without a referral?
gynecology_page.a3: Yes. A referral is not required; book by phone or through an available messenger.
gynecology_page.q4: Can gynecologic surgery be performed laparoscopically?
gynecology_page.a4: Many gynecologic operations can be performed laparoscopically. Suitability depends on the diagnosis, extent of surgery, and the patient’s condition.
gynecology_page.q5: Can the uterus be preserved when treating fibroids?
gynecology_page.a5: In many cases, myomectomy can remove fibroids while preserving the uterus. The decision depends on fibroid size, number and location, symptoms, and reproductive plans.
gynecology_page.q6: Which symptoms require urgent medical attention?
gynecology_page.a6: Seek urgent medical care for severe pain, heavy bleeding, fainting, high fever, or sudden deterioration.
gynecology_page.q7: What is colposcopy and when is it needed?
gynecology_page.a7: Colposcopy examines the cervix under magnification. It may be recommended after an abnormal Pap test, HPV detection, or identification of a suspicious cervical area.
gynecology_page.q8: Can I become pregnant after gynecologic laparoscopy?
gynecology_page.a8: Pregnancy is possible in many cases. Timing depends on the diagnosis, extent of surgery, and recovery and should be discussed with the treating physician.
gynecology_page.q9: Which tests are needed before gynecologic surgery?
gynecology_page.a9: Requirements are individualized and commonly include blood tests, coagulation assessment, ECG, and imaging; additional studies are ordered when indicated.
gynecology_page.q10: How long is recovery after gynecologic surgery?
gynecology_page.a10: Recovery depends on the type and extent of surgery, surgical approach, and the patient’s condition. The surgeon provides individual restrictions.
gynecology_page.q11: What is endometriosis and how is it treated?
gynecology_page.a11: Endometriosis is a condition in which tissue similar to the uterine lining is found outside the uterine cavity. Treatment is individualized.

myomectomy_page.title: Myomectomy: Preserving the Uterus
myomectomy_page.intro: Remove Fibroids and Preserve the Uterus
myomectomy_page.cta: Preserve the Uterus — Book a Consultation
myomectomy_page.q1: Can I become pregnant after myomectomy?
myomectomy_page.a1: Pregnancy after myomectomy is possible, but timing depends on the extent of surgery and uterine healing. Your treating physician should advise you.
myomectomy_page.q2: Should an asymptomatic fibroid be removed?
myomectomy_page.a2: Small asymptomatic fibroids can often be monitored. Surgery depends on growth, bleeding, pain, anemia, pressure symptoms, and reproductive plans.
myomectomy_page.a3: New fibroids may develop after existing nodules are removed. Risk depends on age, number of nodules, and other factors; follow-up is recommended.
myomectomy_page.q4: Which is better: laparoscopy or open surgery?
myomectomy_page.a4: The method is selected individually according to fibroid size, number and location, previous surgery, and pregnancy plans.
myomectomy_page.q5: Can I have a vaginal birth after myomectomy?
myomectomy_page.a5: The mode of delivery depends on the depth of uterine incisions, scar location, and pregnancy course. The obstetrician should make this decision.
myomectomy_page.q6: How does myomectomy differ from hysterectomy?
myomectomy_page.a6: Myomectomy removes fibroids while preserving the uterus. Hysterectomy removes the uterus. Choosing the procedure requires individual assessment.
myomectomy_page.a7: The decision depends not only on size but also growth, symptoms, location, anemia, pressure on adjacent organs, and reproductive plans.
myomectomy_page.a8: Duration depends on the number, size, and location of fibroids, surgical approach, and complexity. The surgeon estimates this after evaluation.
myomectomy_page.q9: How long is recovery after myomectomy?
myomectomy_page.a9: Recovery depends on laparoscopic or open access and the extent of surgery. Individual instructions are provided at discharge.
myomectomy_page.q10: Which fibroid treatment methods are available?
myomectomy_page.a10: Options may include observation, medication, hysteroscopic, laparoscopic or open myomectomy, and other approaches. Selection is individualized.
myomectomy_page.q11: When do fibroids require urgent care?
myomectomy_page.a11: Seek urgent care for severe pain, heavy bleeding, fainting, marked weakness, or rapid deterioration.

checkup_page.title: Medical Checkups and Certificates in Kharkiv
checkup_page.visit: A Queue-Free Checkup in One Visit
checkup_page.q1: Do I need to book a medical checkup in advance?
checkup_page.a1: Yes. MARIA Medical Center works by appointment so you can be seen at the scheduled time without queues.
checkup_page.q2: Which documents are required for a medical checkup?
checkup_page.a2: A passport or another identity document. Corporate examinations may also require an employer referral stating the occupation and workplace factors.
checkup_page.q3: How long does a medical checkup take?
checkup_page.a3: Depending on the type of examination, from 30 minutes to 2–3 hours. A basic checkup usually takes about one hour; a comprehensive checkup may require two visits.
checkup_page.q4: Can an entire team receive corporate checkups?
checkup_page.a4: Yes. MARIA Medical Center works with companies and organizations. Terms and schedules are arranged in advance.
checkup_page.q5: Which documents are needed for a driving medical certificate?
checkup_page.a5: A passport and a 3×4 photograph are required. Confirm the current examination list and certificate validity when booking.
checkup_page.q6: How long is a medical certificate valid?
checkup_page.a6: Validity depends on the certificate type, category, and current regulations. Confirm it when the certificate is issued.

diagnostics_page.title: Cancer Screening in Kharkiv
diagnostics_page.q1: At what age should cancer screening begin?
diagnostics_page.a1: The starting age and frequency depend on sex, family history, and risk factors. A physician should prepare an individual plan.
diagnostics_page.q2: What are tumor markers and how informative are they?
diagnostics_page.a2: Tumor markers are substances that may change in malignant and non-malignant conditions. They cannot establish a diagnosis on their own and must be interpreted with other clinical data.
diagnostics_page.q3: What should I do if a relative had cancer?
diagnostics_page.a3: Tell your physician which relative was affected and at what age. Family history may require earlier or more frequent screening.
diagnostics_page.q4: Can I undergo screening without a referral?
diagnostics_page.a4: Yes. During consultation, the physician will assess risk factors and determine the appropriate examinations.
diagnostics_page.q5: Which tests are included in cancer screening?
diagnostics_page.a5: There is no universal panel for everyone. Tests are selected according to age, sex, symptoms, and risks and may include imaging, endoscopy, and laboratory studies.
diagnostics_page.q6: What should I do if a tumor marker is elevated?
diagnostics_page.a6: Do not panic; consult a physician. An elevated result does not equal cancer and requires professional interpretation and, when appropriate, further evaluation.

expert_hub_page.title: Professor Sushkov Expert Hub
expert_hub_page.subtitle: Oncologic surgery, diagnostics, second opinions, and expert assessment of complex cases.

imaging_page.title: Expert CT and MRI Assessment by an Oncologic Surgeon
imaging_page.lead: A surgical perspective on imaging results as a supplement to the official radiology report.

histology_page.title: Expert Interpretation of a Pathology Report
histology_page.lead: A clear explanation of terminology and its possible relevance to further management.

disclaimer_page.title: Medical Disclaimer
disclaimer_page.subtitle: Important information about medical content on this website
disclaimer_page.warning_box: Website information is provided for educational purposes and is not medical advice.
disclaimer_page.section1_title: 1. The Website Does Not Replace Medical Consultation
disclaimer_page.section1_text: Website materials are informational and educational and do not constitute a diagnosis, prescription, or individualized recommendation. Decisions require assessment by a qualified professional.
disclaimer_page.section2_text: Do not start or stop medicines, procedures, or treatment based on website content. Discuss medical decisions with your treating physician.
disclaimer_page.section3_title: 3. Seek Emergency Care for Life-Threatening Conditions
disclaimer_page.section3_text1: For severe pain, heavy bleeding, loss of consciousness, breathing difficulty, or another threatening condition, contact emergency medical services immediately.
disclaimer_page.section3_emergency: Emergency numbers in Ukraine: 103 and 112. Do not delay urgent care.
disclaimer_page.section4_text: Testimonials, clinical examples, and other patients’ outcomes do not guarantee the same result. Outcomes depend on diagnosis, stage, health, and other factors.
disclaimer_page.section5_text: Website materials are protected by copyright or used lawfully. Copying or commercial use may require permission from the rights holder.

article_cholecystectomy.title: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy
article_cholecystectomy.p1: Cholecystectomy is commonly considered for symptomatic gallstones, acute or chronic cholecystitis, and selected polyps or other gallbladder changes. A surgeon determines indications after evaluation.
article_cholecystectomy.p2: Asymptomatic gallstones do not always require surgery. The decision depends on stone and gallbladder features, comorbidities, and individual complication risk.
article_cholecystectomy.p3: The operation is usually performed under general anesthesia through several small access points. Technique and duration depend on anatomy and case complexity.
article_cholecystectomy.p4: Hospital stay and return to normal activity are individual and depend on the operation, patient condition, and type of work.
article_cholecystectomy.p5: Laparoscopic access is widely used for gallbladder disease but is not suitable for every patient. Benefits, risks, and alternatives should be discussed with a surgeon.
article_cholecystectomy.callout: Book a consultation to determine indications and an appropriate treatment approach.

laparoscopic-cholecystectomy_article.lead: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is a common method of removing the gallbladder through small access points. Surgery is considered after evaluating symptoms, tests, and individual risks.

article_ercp.title: ERCP and Cholecystectomy: Different Roles
article_ercp.p1: ERCP is an endoscopic radiologic procedure used to treat selected disorders of the bile and pancreatic ducts. It is now generally used primarily for therapy rather than diagnosis alone.
article_ercp.p2: ERCP may be considered for common bile duct stones, obstruction, strictures, or drainage. Interventions may include stone removal or stent placement.
article_ercp.p3: Cholecystectomy removes the gallbladder, while ERCP treats the bile ducts. These are different procedures that may complement each other.
article_ercp.p4: Surgery is commonly considered for symptomatic gallstone disease, cholecystitis, and selected other changes. Indications are individualized.
article_ercp.p5: A common bile duct stone with gallbladder disease may require duct treatment and subsequent cholecystectomy. Sequence and timing depend on urgency and patient condition.
article_ercp.callout: Seek urgent medical care for severe pain, fever, jaundice, vomiting, or deterioration.

ercp-cholecystectomy_article.lead: ERCP treats problems in the bile ducts, while cholecystectomy removes the gallbladder. Selection and sequence depend on diagnosis and clinical context.

article_multimodal.title: Multimodal Analgesia and ERAS
article_multimodal.p1: A multimodal approach combines pain-control methods acting through different mechanisms. The goal is adequate analgesia while limiting adverse effects of individual medicines when possible.
article_multimodal.p2: Components are individualized according to surgery, age, kidney and liver function, bleeding risk, allergies, and other factors. Not every medicine is suitable for every patient.
article_multimodal.p3: ERAS is a set of evidence-based perioperative measures before, during, and after surgery. The protocol depends on the procedure and clinical setting.
article_multimodal.p4: Elements may include patient education, nutritional optimization, complication prevention, early mobilization, and resuming oral intake when safe.
article_multimodal.p5: The surgeon and anesthesiologist develop the analgesia and recovery plan. Report medical conditions, allergies, and every medicine you take.
article_multimodal.callout: Discuss a personalized analgesia and recovery plan with your surgeon and anesthesiologist.

multimodal-analgesia_article.lead: Modern postoperative pain control may combine medicines and non-drug methods. The plan requires individual assessment and clinical monitoring.

article_myomectomy.title: Myomectomy and Uterine Preservation
article_myomectomy.p1: Myomectomy removes fibroids while preserving the uterus. Surgery may be considered for symptoms, uterine cavity distortion, growth, anemia, pressure symptoms, or reproductive problems.
article_myomectomy.p2: The method depends on fibroid location, size and number, previous operations, and pregnancy plans. Hysteroscopic, laparoscopic, or open approaches may be used.
article_myomectomy.p3: During laparoscopy, fibroids are removed through small access points and the uterine wall is repaired. Suitability and technique are determined after imaging.
article_myomectomy.p4: Hospital stay, recovery, and timing of pregnancy depend on incision depth, number of fibroids, approach, and healing. Individual advice should involve the surgeon and obstetrician-gynecologist.
article_myomectomy.p5: Myomectomy preserves the uterus but does not guarantee pregnancy or prevent new fibroids. Benefits, risks, and alternatives require individual discussion.
article_myomectomy.callout: If fibroids are found and reproductive plans matter, discuss treatment options with a gynecologist and surgeon.

myomectomy_article.lead: Myomectomy removes fibroids while preserving the uterus. Procedure choice depends on symptoms, fibroid anatomy, and reproductive plans.

article_preparation.title: Preparing for Laparoscopic Surgery
article_preparation.p1: Required tests depend on the operation, age, comorbidities, and clinic requirements. The surgeon and anesthesiologist determine the list.
article_preparation.p2: Tell the team about every medicine and supplement. Do not stop anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, diabetes medicines, or other treatments without medical instructions.
article_preparation.p3: The anesthesiologist and clinic set fasting and fluid rules. Follow the instructions provided, as timing may depend on the operation and your condition.
article_preparation.p4: Bowel preparation is not required for every laparoscopy. Enemas or laxatives should be used only when prescribed by the medical team.
article_preparation.p5: Anxiety before surgery is normal. Discuss the procedure, anesthesia, risks, and recovery. Do not take sedatives without medical approval.
article_preparation.callout: Use this material as a question list and follow the personalized instructions from your surgeon and anesthesiologist.

laparoscopy-preparation_article.lead: Preparation reduces manageable surgical risks but is not identical for everyone. Individual instructions take priority over general advice.

article_screening.title: Cancer Screening and Early Detection
article_screening.p1: Screening examines people without symptoms to detect certain diseases early. Appropriate tests depend on age, sex, family history, and risk factors.
article_screening.p2: Screening benefit is not established for every cancer. Programs exist for breast, cervical, colorectal, and lung cancer in selected risk groups.
article_screening.h2_who: Who Should Be Screened and How Often?
article_screening.p3: There is no universal schedule. A physician considers age, history, smoking, hereditary risk, and previous results.
article_screening.p4: Consultation assesses individual risk and selects appropriate examinations. Tumor markers alone cannot confirm or exclude cancer.
article_screening.p5: Do not wait for symptoms when preventive screening is recommended. Discuss a personal plan and the benefits and harms of each test with a physician.
article_screening.callout: Screening is individualized and does not replace diagnostic evaluation when symptoms are present.

oncology-screening_article.lead: Earlier detection of some cancers can broaden treatment options. Screening should follow evidence-based recommendations and individual risk.

article_author.name: Professor Sushkov
article_author.credentials: Doctor of Medical Sciences, Oncologic Surgeon
article_author.bio: 40 years of clinical experience in surgery and oncologic surgery.

article_cta.title: Book a Consultation
article_cta.subtitle: Discuss individual risks and appropriate examinations with a physician.
